items.q: Why is the global energy transition slower than many expect?
items.correct: Existing fossil-fuel infrastructure is sunk cost, low-carbon alternatives need vast capital, intermittency requires storage, and political/economic interests resist change — even as costs of renewables fall.
items.misconception: The energy transition is slow because renewable energy doesn't actually work.
items.random_wrong: The transition is slow because people don't want to give up cars.
items.why: Renewables are now often cheaper than fossil fuels per unit. The barriers are infrastructure, finance, grid integration and politics — a system inertia problem, not a technology limitation.

items.q: Why does longshore drift move sediment in only one direction along a coast?
items.correct: The prevailing wind drives swash diagonally up the beach at an angle, but gravity-driven backwash returns sediment straight downhill — creating net movement in the swash direction.
items.misconception: Longshore drift moves sediment back and forth with the tide.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift only happens during storms.
items.why: It's wave-driven, not tide-driven. The net direction is set by the prevailing wind angle relative to the coast — that's why groynes are placed perpendicular to interrupt it.

items.q: What is the ITCZ and why does it bring rain?
items.correct: The Intertropical Convergence Zone is where trade winds from both hemispheres converge near the equator, forcing air to rise, cool and produce heavy convectional rainfall.
items.misconception: The ITCZ is a fixed band of permanent storms at the equator.
items.random_wrong: The ITCZ is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The ITCZ shifts seasonally north and south with the sun, driving monsoon rains. Students treat it as static — its movement explains why tropical regions have wet/dry seasons.
The answer examiners want is: Average weather over many years. “Today's weather” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Rain gauge. “Thermometer” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Over how many years is climate usually averaged?
items.why: Exact answer: 30. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
